_meta.description: Retirement city dataset seed. Scores are 1-10 (10 = best). Monthly costs in USD for a single person, comfortable lifestyle.

cities.description: The Himalayan kingdom's spiritual capital — Boudhanath stupa, Pashupatinath temple, trekking gateways to Everest and Annapurna, and some of Asia's most affordable urban living.
cities.visa_notes: Nepal offers a Tourist Visa extendable up to 150 days per year. No dedicated retirement visa exists. Most retirees use successive tourist visa renewals or overland border runs. No income or capital gains tax on foreign-sourced income.
cities.healthcare_notes: B&B Hospital and CIWEC Clinic (expat-focused) are Kathmandu's best. Serious conditions require evacuation to Bangkok or Delhi.
best_for: Trekking and adventure lovers
Spiritual seekers (Hindu/Buddhist heritage)
Ultra-budget retirees
avoid_if: Those needing quality healthcare locally
Those sensitive to air pollution (Kathmandu Valley smog is severe seasonally)

cities.description: Nepal's lakeside adventure capital — Phewa Lake reflecting the Annapurna massif, paragliding, the Annapurna Circuit trailhead, and a laid-back traveler culture at ultra-low costs.
cities.visa_notes: Same Tourist Visa framework as Kathmandu. Pokhara is more peaceful and less polluted than Kathmandu, making it increasingly popular with long-term expats.
cities.healthcare_notes: Manipal Teaching Hospital provides basic care. Serious conditions require Kathmandu (6–8 hrs by road) or air evacuation to Bangkok.
best_for: Those wanting Himalayan scenery without Kathmandu's chaos
Trekkers using it as a base
Paragliding and adventure enthusiasts
avoid_if: Those needing reliable internet or medical care
Retirees wanting urban amenities

cities.description: One of the world's most densely populated megacities — a chaotic, vibrant Delta capital with ultra-cheap living, extraordinary street food, and the gateway to the Sundarbans mangrove forest.
cities.visa_notes: Bangladesh offers On-Arrival Visa (30 days) for most Western nationalities. Long-term stays require a Bangladesh Visa from embassy. No retirement visa program exists. Bureaucracy can be challenging.
cities.healthcare_notes: Square Hospital and Apollo Hospitals Dhaka provide the best private care. Standards well below Western levels; serious cases go to Bangkok or Singapore.
best_for: Adventure retirees wanting the most off-the-beaten-path South Asia experience
Those with Bangladeshi heritage
avoid_if: Most retirees — extreme traffic, pollution, flooding risk, and limited infrastructure make Dhaka very challenging

cities.description: Pakistan's purpose-built modern capital — clean, tree-lined, with the Margalla Hills as a backdrop, a more relaxed pace than Karachi or Lahore, and very low costs for the quality of life available.
cities.visa_notes: Pakistan offers an e-Visa and On-Arrival Visa for many nationalities. Long-stay requires a Pakistan Resident Permit. Pakistan launched visa-on-arrival for 50+ countries in 2019. Security situation requires careful research.
cities.healthcare_notes: Shifa International Hospital and Pakistan Institute of Medical Sciences (PIMS) provide the best care in the capital. Private sector standards are acceptable for most needs.
best_for: Those with Pakistani heritage
Adventurous retirees with South Asia connections
avoid_if: Most Western retirees — security concerns, bureaucratic complexity, and limited expat infrastructure make this a specialist destination

cities.description: Pakistan's cultural soul and Mughal capital — the Lahore Fort, Badshahi Mosque, legendary food street, and a city whose hospitality and history rival any in South Asia.
cities.visa_notes: Pakistan e-Visa / On-Arrival framework. Lahore is more culturally immersive than Islamabad but less convenient for foreign bureaucratic processes.
cities.healthcare_notes: Shaukat Khanum Cancer Hospital (world-class oncology) and Services Hospital provide the best care. Shaukat Khanum is genuinely excellent by any standard.
best_for: History and Mughal architecture lovers
Those with Punjabi heritage
Food culture adventurers
avoid_if: Most Western retirees given security concerns, air quality (frequently worst in the world), and infrastructure challenges

cities.description: Pakistan's chaotic coastal megacity and financial capital — the Arabian Sea coast, extraordinary street food, a kaleidoscopic cultural mix, and living costs among Asia's very lowest.
cities.visa_notes: Pakistan e-Visa / On-Arrival framework. Karachi's security situation has improved significantly since 2013 but remains a serious consideration.
cities.healthcare_notes: Aga Khan University Hospital is genuinely world-class and one of South Asia's finest institutions. Private sector generally better than public.
best_for: Those with Karachi heritage
Adventurous retirees comfortable with large developing-world megacity life
avoid_if: Almost all Western retirees — security, traffic, pollution, and infrastructure make this an extremely challenging choice

cities.description: Kazakhstan's cultural and financial capital beneath the Tian Shan mountains — a surprisingly cosmopolitan city with excellent ski resorts, growing expat scene, and visa-free entry for most Western nationalities.
cities.visa_notes: Kazakhstan offers 30-day visa-free entry for most Western nationalities and up to 1-year e-Visa for many others. Long-term residence permits are available. Kazakhstan's growing economy and strategic position attract a small but growing expat community.
cities.healthcare_notes: MedClinic and Interteach hospitals provide the best private care. Government facilities vary significantly. Serious cases go to Moscow, Istanbul, or Bangkok.
best_for: Skiing enthusiasts (Shymbulak resort)
Those wanting a Central Asian adventure city
USD/EUR income earners
avoid_if: Those wanting warm weather (harsh winters)
Retirees needing reliable high-quality healthcare

cities.description: Central Asia's largest city — Soviet-wide boulevards, stunning Islamic architecture in nearby Samarkand and Bukhara, emerging hospitality infrastructure, and very low costs.
cities.visa_notes: Uzbekistan offers 30-day visa-free entry for 90+ countries and e-Visa for most others. No dedicated retirement visa. Uzbekistan has been rapidly liberalizing since 2016 with growing tourist and expat infrastructure.
cities.healthcare_notes: International SOS clinic and private clinics in Tashkent provide expat-quality basic care. Advanced care requires medical evacuation to Istanbul or Moscow.
best_for: Silk Road history enthusiasts
Budget adventurers
Those wanting to experience rapid economic transformation
avoid_if: Those needing reliable healthcare
Retirees wanting familiar Western comforts

cities.description: The Tian Shan's compact capital — visa-free for most Western nationalities, extraordinary mountain trekking access, Soviet-era parks, and some of Asia's lowest costs of living.
cities.visa_notes: Kyrgyzstan offers visa-free entry for 60+ countries and e-Visa for most others. Very accessible. No income tax on foreign-sourced income. Growing nomadic lifestyle and digital nomad community.
cities.healthcare_notes: Intermed and private Kyrgyz-Turkish Manas hospitals provide basic care. Serious cases go to Almaty or abroad.
best_for: Extreme-budget adventurers
Trekkers and mountaineers
Those wanting nomadic culture access (yurt stays, horse trekking)
avoid_if: Those needing any medical infrastructure
Retirees wanting modern urban amenities

cities.description: The world's most ambitious city — the Burj Khalifa, year-round sun, zero income tax, world-class malls and beaches, and an expatriate lifestyle that defines 21st-century Gulf living.
cities.visa_notes: UAE Retirement Visa (valid 5 years, renewable) available for those 55+ meeting one of: property worth AED 2M+, savings of AED 1M+, or monthly income of AED 20,000+. No income tax. Dubai is the world's leading expat city.
cities.healthcare_notes: Cleveland Clinic Abu Dhabi, Mediclinic, and American Hospital Dubai provide world-class care. JCI-accredited hospitals are numerous.
best_for: Affluent tax-conscious retirees
Those wanting world-class English-language infrastructure
Beach and luxury lifestyle seekers
avoid_if: Budget retirees
Those who dislike extreme summer heat (50°C+)
Anyone prioritizing cultural authenticity over modernity

cities.description: The UAE's dignified capital — the Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque, the Louvre Abu Dhabi, Yas Island's F1 circuit, and a more relaxed, family-oriented Gulf lifestyle than Dubai.
cities.visa_notes: UAE Retirement Visa (5-year, renewable) — same criteria as Dubai. Cleveland Clinic is in Abu Dhabi specifically, making healthcare even stronger here than Dubai.
cities.healthcare_notes: Cleveland Clinic Abu Dhabi is one of the world's best hospitals, full stop. Sheikh Khalifa Medical City also outstanding.
best_for: Those wanting Dubai's benefits with more cultural substance
Healthcare-focused affluent retirees
Art and culture lovers
avoid_if: Budget retirees
Those wanting a walkable, car-free environment

cities.description: The Gulf's cultural ambition incarnate — the Museum of Islamic Art, Al Zubarah UNESCO fort, world-class dining, zero income tax, and a 2022 FIFA World Cup legacy of infrastructure.
cities.visa_notes: Qatar Residence Permit for property owners (The Pearl) or through sponsorship. Qatar now offers 5-year multiple-entry tourist visas for 80+ countries. No income tax.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hamad Medical Corporation is Qatar's public system and genuinely world-class. Sidra Medicine (Boston Children's Hospital affiliate) is outstanding.
best_for: Affluent retirees wanting Gulf lifestyle with cultural depth
Those valuing world-class safety
Museum and art lovers
avoid_if: Budget retirees
Those sensitive to extreme summer heat
Those wanting casual outdoor lifestyles

cities.description: The Gulf's hidden gem — a clean, safe, and architecturally coherent Arab capital with dramatic mountain wadis, turtle nesting beaches, souk culture, and a far more affordable cost than Dubai.
cities.visa_notes: Oman Retirement Residency requires OMR 4,000/month income or OMR 250,000 in savings, plus property ownership. Most Western nationalities get 30-day visa-on-arrival extendable. No income tax.
cities.healthcare_notes: Royal Hospital and Khoula Hospital are the leading public facilities. Muscat Private Hospital and IMC provide strong private care.
best_for: Those wanting Gulf living at lower cost
Outdoor and wadi explorers
Those wanting authentic Arab culture with safety
avoid_if: Budget retirees (Gulf costs apply)
Those wanting Dubai's cosmopolitan energy

cities.description: The Levant's modern Arab capital — the Roman Citadel, Petra two hours away, extraordinary hummus and mansaf, a safe and cosmopolitan atmosphere, and a growing expat retiree scene.
cities.visa_notes: Jordan offers On-Arrival Visa (1–3 months) for most Western nationalities. Long-term residence available via property ownership or the Jordan Retirement Visa (for those with pension income). Jordan has historically been one of the Middle East's most politically stable countries.
cities.healthcare_notes: Jordan University Hospital and private Istishari and Khalidi Hospitals are genuinely strong — Jordan is a major medical tourism destination for the Arab world.
best_for: Middle East adventurers
History and archaeology lovers (Petra, Jerash)
Those wanting an affordable, safe Arab capital
avoid_if: Those wanting coastal living (landlocked with one small Red Sea port)
Budget retirees struggling with JOD (not cheap)

cities.description: The Paris of the Middle East — extraordinary food, multilingual sophistication, Mediterranean beaches backed by cedared mountains, and a city that has survived everything history threw at it.
cities.visa_notes: Lebanon offers 1-month visa-on-arrival for most Western nationalities, extendable. The country is in severe economic crisis since 2019 — banking collapse, currency devaluation, and political instability. USD cash is the de facto currency.
cities.healthcare_notes: American University of Beirut Medical Center (AUBMC) is one of the Middle East's finest academic hospitals. Quality private sector exists but resources are strained by the economic crisis.
best_for: Food and culture lovers willing to manage instability
Those with Lebanese heritage
Adventurous retirees who've researched the situation thoroughly
avoid_if: Those wanting stability, reliable utilities, or financial security
Most Western retirees — the economic and political crisis make this very high-risk

cities.description: The Mediterranean's startup capital — Bauhaus White City (UNESCO), world-class beaches, extraordinary food scene, vibrant LGBTQ+ culture, and one of the Middle East's highest living standards.
cities.visa_notes: Israel allows 3-month visa-free stays for most Western nationalities. Aliyah (Law of Return) allows Jewish retirees to immigrate with full citizenship. Non-Jewish non-citizen retirees face limited long-term visa options. The security situation requires careful assessment.
cities.healthcare_notes: Ichilov (Tel Aviv Sourasky Medical Center) and Sheba Medical Center are world-ranked. Israeli healthcare is widely regarded as the Middle East's finest.
best_for: Jewish retirees making Aliyah
LGBTQ+ retirees
Tech and startup culture enthusiasts
avoid_if: Those concerned about regional security
Budget retirees (one of the world's most expensive cities for its size)

cities.description: The world's most contested sacred city — the Western Wall, the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, the Dome of the Rock, and 3,000 years of civilization layered on every stone.
cities.visa_notes: Same Israeli visa framework as Tel Aviv. Jerusalem carries additional security complexity given its political status. Aliyah route available for Jewish retirees.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hadassah Medical Center (Ein Karem campus) is a world-renowned academic hospital, particularly famous for its Chagall windows and exceptional medical care.
best_for: Those drawn to the world's most spiritually significant city
History and religious pilgrimage enthusiasts
Jewish retirees making Aliyah
avoid_if: Those wanting a relaxed, beach-oriented retirement
Retirees sensitive to political and security tensions

cities.description: Saudi Arabia's rapidly transforming capital — Vision 2030's showpiece, new entertainment venues, Al-Ula desert heritage, and a society changing faster than almost anywhere on Earth.
cities.visa_notes: Saudi Arabia now offers tourist e-Visas for 50+ nationalities. Longer-term residence for retirees typically requires employer sponsorship or the new Saudi Premium Residency (SAR 800,000 one-time fee or SAR 100,000/year). Social norms and laws remain more conservative than Western standards.
cities.healthcare_notes: King Faisal Specialist Hospital and Research Centre is one of the world's finest academic hospitals. Saudi Arabia has invested heavily in healthcare infrastructure.
best_for: Those working in Saudi Arabia retiring in place
Those intrigued by Vision 2030's transformation
High-income earners benefiting from zero income tax
avoid_if: Those wanting social freedoms similar to Western countries
Anyone bothered by extreme summer heat

cities.description: The Gulf's oil-rich original — a wealthy, conservative Arab capital with extraordinary souks, the Arabian Gulf waterfront (the Corniche), and one of the world's highest GDPs per capita.
cities.visa_notes: Kuwait does not offer a retirement visa. Long-term residence typically requires employer sponsorship or family ties. Tourist visas (1 month) are available for most Western nationalities. Limited visa options make Kuwait difficult for independent retirees.
cities.healthcare_notes: Kuwait Hospital and Mubarak Al-Kabeer Hospital are the leading public facilities. Private Dar Al Shifa and American Hospital Kuwait provide better expat-oriented care.
best_for: Those working in Kuwait retiring in place
Those with Kuwaiti family connections
avoid_if: Independent retirees without a visa sponsorship pathway
Those wanting social freedoms or entertainment beyond shopping

cities.description: The Gulf's most liberal capital — Bahrain's small island nation has the most relaxed social laws in the Gulf, a Formula 1 Grand Prix, weekend escape for Saudis, and no income tax.
cities.visa_notes: Bahrain offers a 1-year Retirement Visa for those 55+ with monthly income of $1,500+ or $135,000 in savings — one of the Gulf's most accessible retirement visas. No income tax.
cities.healthcare_notes: Bahrain Defence Force Hospital and American Mission Hospital provide good care. King Hamad University Hospital is the leading facility.
best_for: Those wanting the Gulf's most socially relaxed environment
Financial services retirees
Formula 1 and motorsport enthusiasts
avoid_if: Those wanting lush green landscapes
Budget retirees

cities.description: Cyprus's second coastal city and main gateway — the Hala Sultan Tekke mosque, Finikoudes promenade, salt lake flamingos, and one of Cyprus's most authentically local atmospheres.
cities.visa_notes: Cyprus EU and Schengen. Same Cyprus Category F Retirement Visa framework. Larnaca Airport is the main international gateway, making it very accessible.
cities.healthcare_notes: Larnaca General Hospital provides public care; private Apollonion and Limassol hospitals offer better specialist access.
best_for: Those wanting authentic Cyprus over resort-heavy Limassol
Beach lovers
Budget EU Mediterranean retirees
avoid_if: Those needing full specialist medical care locally
Retirees wanting big-city entertainment

cities.description: One of the world's most densely populated capital islands — a compact Muslim city surrounded by world-famous overwater bungalows, coral reefs, and the Indian Ocean's clearest waters.
cities.visa_notes: Maldives grants 30-day visa-free stays on arrival, extendable to 90 days. No long-term retirement visa exists. Long-term residency typically requires work permit, marriage to a Maldivian, or significant property investment in designated areas. Islam is the state religion.
cities.healthcare_notes: ADK Hospital in Malé provides the best local private care. Serious cases require evacuation to India (Bangalore/Kochi) or Sri Lanka.
best_for: Dive instructors and underwater enthusiasts retiring in place
Those wanting a unique Indian Ocean island base
avoid_if: Most conventional retirees — no retirement visa, limited medical care, and Islamic social restrictions make long-term living challenging

cities.description: Sri Lanka's commercial capital — colonial Dutch and British architecture, superb seafood, a recovering economy post-2022 crisis, and proximity to cultural Kandy and beach Galle at very low costs.
cities.visa_notes: Sri Lanka offers visa-on-arrival or e-Visa (30 days, extendable to 6 months). No formal retirement visa but the 'Retirement Residence Visa' (RRV) is available for those 55+ with $1,500/month income or $15,000 in a Sri Lanka bank account.
cities.healthcare_notes: Asiri Hospital Group and Nawaloka Hospital provide good private care. Serious specialist care may require Chennai or Singapore.
best_for: Budget retirees wanting tropical beauty
Tea and culinary enthusiasts
Those wanting beach access (Galle 2hrs) with city base
avoid_if: Those worried about economic stability (post-2022 crisis recovery ongoing)
Retirees needing advanced specialist care locally

cities.description: The world's only carbon-negative country's capital — Gross National Happiness policy, dzong fortresses, no traffic lights, Buddhist culture at its most intact, and mandatory $250/day tourist fee keeps crowds away.
cities.visa_notes: Bhutan requires a Sustainable Development Fee of USD 100/night (reduced from $250 in 2023) for all foreign tourists. Long-term residence is essentially impossible for non-Indian non-Bhutanese nationals. No retirement visa exists. One of the most restricted nations for long-term foreign residency.
cities.healthcare_notes: JDWNR Hospital is Bhutan's main referral hospital; resources are limited. Serious cases transfer to India.
best_for: Those wanting a profound, unique cultural experience on extended visits
Buddhist practitioners
avoid_if: Anyone wanting to actually retire and live here long-term — this is essentially not possible for most Western nationals

cities.description: The world's coldest capital and gateway to the Gobi Desert and endless steppes — Genghis Khan's homeland, a growing expat community around the mining boom, and one of Asia's most unique lifestyles.
cities.visa_notes: Mongolia offers 30-day visa-free entry for 60+ nationalities. Extendable residence permits available. No income tax on foreign-sourced income.
cities.healthcare_notes: SOS Medica Mongolia clinic and Intermed Hospital provide basic expat-quality care. Serious cases require Beijing or Seoul.
best_for: The most adventurous retirees on earth
Nomadic culture enthusiasts
Horseback and outdoor extreme travelers
avoid_if: Almost everyone — winters reach -40°C, air quality in Ulaanbaatar is among the world's worst (coal-burning gers), and infrastructure is limited

cities.description: Cambodia's complex, rising capital — the Royal Palace, the Mekong-Tonle Sap confluence, a rapidly developing riverfront, and very easy retirement visa access at some of Asia's lowest costs.
cities.visa_notes: Cambodia's Ordinary Visa (Type E) is extendable indefinitely for $290/year — making it one of Asia's easiest long-term retirement visa solutions. USD is freely used alongside KHR. No income or capital gains tax on foreign-sourced income.
cities.healthcare_notes: Royal Phnom Penh Hospital and Calmette Hospital provide basic care. Raffles Medical Phnom Penh offers better private care. Serious cases go to Bangkok (1 hr by air).
best_for: Ultra-budget retirees
Those wanting Asia's easiest retirement visa
Angkor Wat and Khmer culture enthusiasts
avoid_if: Those needing reliable medical care locally
Retirees wanting stable infrastructure and modern services

cities.description: Asia's most laid-back capital — Buddhist temples, baguettes from French colonial days, the Mekong at sunset, and a sleepy pace that makes everything feel like it's happening in slow motion.
cities.visa_notes: Laos offers 30-day visa-on-arrival or e-Visa for most nationalities. The Laos Retirement Visa (for 50+) requires $3,000/month income or $150,000 in a Lao bank. Typical expats use 30-day tourist visas with renewals or border runs to Thailand.
cities.healthcare_notes: Mahosot Hospital and Setthathirath Hospital are the main facilities. Medical evacuation to Bangkok (1 hr) is strongly recommended for serious conditions.
best_for: Those wanting the slowest, most peaceful Asian capital
Budget retirees wanting near-zero stress living
Those happy with simple tropical lifestyle
avoid_if: Those needing any real medical infrastructure
Retirees wanting activity or cultural depth beyond Buddhist temples

cities.description: The world's most surreal capital — a purpose-built city of 20-lane empty highways, golf courses, and virtually no traffic, built by the military junta and now under continued military control post-2021 coup.
cities.visa_notes: Myanmar is under military control following the February 2021 coup. Most Western governments advise against travel. Tourist visas are technically available but the political and security situation makes retirement here completely inadvisable.
cities.healthcare_notes: Naypyidaw General Hospital is the main facility but resources are extremely limited. The post-coup crisis has severely strained healthcare infrastructure.
best_for: No one. Not a recommended retirement destination under any current circumstances.
avoid_if: Everyone — Myanmar's military junta, ongoing civil conflict, international sanctions, and lack of any expat infrastructure make this entirely unsuitable for retirees

cities.description: The oil-rich sultanate's capital — the Omar Ali Saifuddien Mosque, the world's largest residential palace, zero income tax, and a quiet, conservative Islamic lifestyle subsidized by oil wealth.
cities.visa_notes: Brunei offers 14–30 day visa-free stays for most Western nationalities. Long-term residence typically requires employment with a Bruneian company or family sponsorship. Very limited retirement visa pathway. Alcohol is illegal for Muslims; non-Muslims can import limited personal quantities.
cities.healthcare_notes: RIPAS Hospital (Raja Isteri Pengiran Anak Saleha) is a modern, well-funded public hospital. Care is generally adequate and free for residents.
best_for: Those working in Brunei's oil sector retiring in place
Those wanting an exceptionally safe, quiet lifestyle
avoid_if: Those wanting social freedoms or nightlife (effectively none)
Independent retirees without a visa sponsorship pathway

cities.description: Southeast Asia's newest nation's capital — a frontier city still building itself on the Timor Sea, extraordinary diving, Portuguese colonial churches, and a people of exceptional warmth.
cities.visa_notes: Timor-Leste offers 30-day visa-on-arrival extendable once. No retirement visa. Long-term stays require business or NGO sponsorship. USD is the official currency. Infrastructure is very basic.
cities.healthcare_notes: Guido Valadares National Hospital is severely under-resourced. Medical evacuation to Darwin, Australia (1 hr) or Bali is essential for anything serious.
best_for: NGO workers, adventurers, and divers wanting a true frontier experience
avoid_if: Virtually all retirees — infrastructure, healthcare, and services are far below any standard that supports comfortable retirement

cities.description: The Caribbean's time-frozen capital — crumbling Baroque palaces, 1950s Chevrolets, salsa on every corner, mojitos, and one of the world's most unique and complex societies to experience as a long-term resident.
cities.visa_notes: Cuba's legal residency process for foreigners is extremely complex and rarely granted for retirement purposes. Most long-term stays use a successive tourist card (Tarjeta del Turista) system with exits required every 30–90 days. US citizens face additional restrictions due to the embargo. Not straightforward for retirement.
cities.healthcare_notes: Cuba's public healthcare system has strong fundamentals at the primary level but chronic shortages of medicines and equipment. La Cira-García hospital in Havana provides better care for foreigners (payment in hard currency).
best_for: Spanish-speaking adventurers wanting the world's most unique Caribbean experience
Music and culture aficionados
avoid_if: Those needing reliable internet, modern infrastructure, or US dollar banking
US citizens (legal complications)
Retirees wanting stability and comfort

cities.description: Melanesia's largest city and one of the world's most biodiverse nations' gateway — extraordinary tribal cultures, natural wonders, and significant expatriate infrastructure from the mining/LNG sector.
cities.visa_notes: Papua New Guinea requires visas for most nationalities. Retirement visa options are very limited. Expatriates typically enter via business or employment visas.
cities.healthcare_notes: Port Moresby General Hospital is the national referral hospital but severely under-resourced. Private Pacific International Hospital is significantly better. Evacuation to Brisbane or Cairns is essential for serious cases.
best_for: Mining and resource-sector retirees in place
Extreme adventure travelers with extensive PNG experience
avoid_if: Most retirees — crime, poor infrastructure, limited healthcare, and extreme heat/humidity make Port Moresby very challenging

cities.description: Guadalcanal's WWII-storied capital — stunning Pacific diving, traditional Melanesian culture, a growing Chinese economic presence, and one of the South Pacific's most authentic small capitals.
cities.visa_notes: Solomon Islands offers 90-day visa-free entry for most nationalities. Long-term residence requires a permit. Limited infrastructure for independent retirees.
cities.healthcare_notes: National Referral Hospital is severely under-resourced. Medical evacuation to Brisbane or Port Moresby for all but basic care.
best_for: WWII history enthusiasts (Guadalcanal campaign)
Divers wanting remote, pristine Pacific reefs
avoid_if: Almost all retirees — healthcare, infrastructure, internet, and services are far below any comfortable retirement standard

cities.description: The South Pacific's administrative capital — Fiji's cultural and political heart, lush rainforest, and access to the famous Coral Coast, while offering more infrastructure than Fiji's resort islands.
cities.visa_notes: Fiji offers 4-month visa-free stays for most Western nationalities. Fiji's Retirement Visa (for 55+) requires FJD 2,000/month income or FJD 200,000 in savings, plus health insurance. One of the Pacific's most accessible retirement visas.
cities.healthcare_notes: Colonial War Memorial Hospital (CWM) is Fiji's main public hospital — resource-constrained. Private Pacific Private Hospital provides better care. Serious cases go to Australia or New Zealand.
best_for: Pacific island lifestyle seekers
Those wanting Fiji's friendliness with more infrastructure than resort islands
avoid_if: Those needing reliable healthcare
Those wanting Western comfort levels

cities.description: The Kingdom of Tonga's royal capital — the only Pacific island nation never colonized, whale swimming season (July–October), friendly Polynesian culture, and a very small-town Pacific pace.
cities.visa_notes: Tonga offers 31-day visa-free entry for most nationalities, extendable to 6 months. No formal retirement visa. Long-term residence requires business investment or family ties.
cities.healthcare_notes: Vaiola Hospital is the main public facility — very limited resources. Medical evacuation to Auckland (3 hrs) for serious cases.
best_for: Whale-watching and ocean enthusiasts
Those wanting the most authentic, uncorrupted Polynesian experience
avoid_if: Those needing medical care
Retirees wanting modern amenities or reliable internet

cities.description: Polynesia's most authentic capital — Robert Louis Stevenson's final home, Saturday flea market, fale beach houses, and a deeply communal Samoan fa'a Samoa culture of exceptional warmth.
cities.visa_notes: Samoa offers 60-day visa-free entry for most nationalities, extendable to 12 months. Long-term residence via Investment or work permits. Growing diasporic connections with New Zealand and Australia.
cities.healthcare_notes: Tupua Tamasese Meaole Hospital is the national referral hospital with very limited resources. Medical evacuation to Auckland for serious cases.
best_for: Those wanting the most authentic Polynesian culture
Literature enthusiasts (Stevenson's Vailima estate)
avoid_if: Those needing healthcare infrastructure
Retirees wanting modern amenities

cities.description: The South Pacific's most accessible second passport hub — Vanuatu's Citizenship by Investment program is one of the fastest in the world, French and English co-official, active volcanoes, and diving.
cities.visa_notes: Vanuatu offers 30-day visa-free entry for most nationalities. Vanuatu's Citizenship by Investment program (from $130,000 USD donation) is one of the world's fastest — 60-day processing. Long-term residence available. No income, capital gains, or inheritance tax.
cities.healthcare_notes: Vila Central Hospital has very limited resources. Pacific International Hospital offers private care. Medical evacuation to Brisbane or Noumea for serious cases.
best_for: Second citizenship/passport seekers
Divers (world-class wrecks and reefs)
Those wanting zero tax jurisdiction with Pacific lifestyle
avoid_if: Those needing healthcare infrastructure
Retirees wanting reliable internet or modern services

cities.description: The world's fourth-smallest and most climate-vulnerable nation — an atoll capital at risk of disappearing beneath rising seas, a place of extraordinary remoteness and community life.
cities.visa_notes: Tuvalu offers 1-month visa-on-arrival for most nationalities. No retirement visa. Infrastructure is almost non-existent beyond basic government services.
cities.healthcare_notes: Princess Margaret Hospital is a very small facility with extremely limited resources. Medical evacuation to Fiji or New Zealand for any real medical issue.
best_for: Extreme travelers and climate researchers wanting to experience a nation under existential threat
avoid_if: All retirees — this is not a viable retirement destination by any conventional metric

cities.description: One of the world's most remote and climate-threatened atolls — the site of a ferocious WWII battle, an extraordinary WWII history, and a nation straddling the dateline.
cities.visa_notes: Kiribati requires a visa for most nationalities. No retirement visa. One of the world's most logistically challenging places to access and live in.
cities.healthcare_notes: Tungaru Central Hospital has minimal resources. No evacuation options are nearby — Fiji or Australia required.
best_for: WWII historians wanting to visit Battle of Tarawa sites
avoid_if: All retirees — not a viable retirement destination

cities.description: The Marshall Islands' atoll capital — nuclear test history (Bikini Atoll nearby), Compact of Free Association with the US giving unique access rights, and some of the Pacific's best diving.
cities.visa_notes: US citizens can enter and live in the Marshall Islands indefinitely under the Compact of Free Association — no visa required. Other nationalities get 90-day visa-free entry. USD is the official currency.
cities.healthcare_notes: Majuro Hospital has very limited resources but is somewhat better than other Pacific micro-states due to US Compact funding. Serious cases go to Hawaii.
best_for: US retirees wanting a unique tax-free Pacific atoll life
Divers and nuclear history researchers
avoid_if: Non-US retirees without Compact advantages
Anyone needing medical care beyond basics

cities.description: A tiny governmental capital on Pohnpei island — lush rainforest, Nan Madol (mysterious ancient city), extraordinary diving at Truk Lagoon nearby, and US Compact free access for Americans.
cities.visa_notes: US citizens have unlimited right of entry under the Compact of Free Association. Other nationalities get 30-day visa-free entry. USD is the official currency.
cities.healthcare_notes: Pohnpei State Hospital has very limited resources. Guam or Hawaii for serious medical issues.
best_for: US retirees wanting extreme Pacific remoteness and diving (Chuuk/Truk Lagoon is the world's top wreck diving site)
avoid_if: Non-US retirees
Those wanting any modern urban amenities

cities.description: The world's smallest island nation and the only country with no official capital — a phosphate-stripped landscape, once the world's richest per capita, now one of the most isolated places on Earth.
cities.visa_notes: Nauru requires a visa in advance for all nationalities — no visa on arrival. Very difficult to obtain. Only Air Nauru (limited schedule) provides access. No retirement visa of any kind.
cities.healthcare_notes: Nauru Hospital has minimal resources. Medical evacuation to Brisbane or Fiji for all real medical needs.
best_for: Extreme collectors of unique destinations (one of the hardest places on Earth to visit)
avoid_if: All retirees without exception

cities.description: Palau's tiny administrative capital — a purpose-built government center on Babeldaob, but Palau's real draw is Koror and its Rock Islands, legendary diving, and extraordinary marine sanctuary.
cities.visa_notes: Palau offers 30-day visa-free entry for most nationalities. Long-term residence requires investment or spousal connection. US Compact means Americans have certain additional rights.
cities.healthcare_notes: Belau National Hospital in Koror has very limited resources. Medical evacuation to Guam or Manila for serious cases.
best_for: World-class divers (Palau's Rock Islands and Jellyfish Lake are bucket-list)
Those wanting a pristine Pacific marine environment
avoid_if: Those needing medical care or modern infrastructure for comfortable long-term retirement

cities.description: Southeast Asia's most overlooked megacity — a chaotic, vibrant capital of 14 million with world-class malls, excellent English, one of Asia's best retiree visa programs, and extraordinary affordability.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV (Special Resident Retiree's Visa) — one of Asia's best retirement visas. For those 50+: $20,000 deposit (with pension) or $10,000 (with $800/month pension). Lifetime renewable, work rights, and multiple entry. The Philippine Retirement Authority (PRA) administers efficiently.
cities.healthcare_notes: Makati Medical Center, St. Luke's Medical Center (BGC), and The Medical City are world-class private hospitals at very affordable rates. Strong JCI-accredited network.
best_for: Those wanting Asia's best retirement visa
English-speaking expats wanting affordable megacity life
Those with Filipino partners or family
avoid_if: Those sensitive to traffic, tropical heat, and occasional typhoons
Retirees wanting quiet small-town living

cities.description: The Visayas' Queen City — the Philippines' oldest city, a booming IT-BPO hub, gateway to Malapascua and Moalboal diving, and a manageable city scale compared to Manila chaos.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies nationwide. Cebu City has its own PRA office. Mactan Island has a large expat community near the beach.
cities.healthcare_notes: Chong Hua Hospital and Cebu Doctors' University Hospital are strong regional medical centers. Affordable private specialist access.
best_for: Those wanting Manila's amenities at smaller scale
Divers (Malapascua, Moalboal)
Those wanting easy beach-to-city balance
avoid_if: Those wanting complete tranquility (Cebu City is still busy)
Those avoiding urban traffic

cities.description: Mindanao's safest and most orderly city — the durian capital of the Philippines, Mount Apo trekking, consistently ranked the Philippines' most peaceful city, and extremely low costs.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. Davao is outside the typhoon belt — a significant advantage for Philippines retirement. Once known as dangerous, it has had consistent safety rankings under strong local governance.
cities.healthcare_notes: Southern Philippines Medical Center and Davao Doctors Hospital provide strong regional care. More affordable than Manila's top hospitals.
best_for: Budget retirees wanting the Philippines' safest city
Those wanting durian, trekking, and tropical nature
Those avoiding typhoon zones
avoid_if: Those wanting Manila's or Cebu's full urban amenity range
Those concerned about Mindanao's historical reputation (Davao itself is safe)

cities.description: The Philippines' most famous beach island — White Beach's powder-white sand and turquoise water, kitesurfing at Bulabog, legendary sunsets, and a resort-town lifestyle that many retirees never leave.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. Boracay requires registration for long-term residents. The island was closed for rehabilitation in 2018 and reopened much cleaner.
cities.healthcare_notes: De Los Santos Medical Center (Boracay) provides basic care; serious cases transfer to Kalibo (Aklan) or Manila by air.
best_for: Beach and water sports retirees
Those wanting a perpetual island vacation lifestyle
Kitesurfers
avoid_if: Those needing medical infrastructure
Those wanting cultural depth beyond resort life

cities.description: The Philippines' last frontier — Puerto Princesa's underground river (UNESCO), El Nido's limestone karsts, world-class coral reefs, and an island of extraordinary nature with very low costs.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. Palawan is outside the typhoon belt's main track. Palawan province has specific environmental regulations on development — keeping it pristine.
cities.healthcare_notes: Ospital ng Palawan handles basic care in Puerto Princesa; El Nido and Coron have very limited medical facilities. Air evacuation to Manila for serious issues.
best_for: Nature lovers and divers
Those wanting the Philippines' most pristine environment
UNESCO heritage and coral reef enthusiasts
avoid_if: Those needing urban amenities or reliable internet outside Puerto Princesa
Retirees needing medical access

cities.description: The Philippines' cool highland retreat south of Manila — Taal Volcano's drama, fresh bulalo beef soup, cooler temperatures than lowland Philippines, and a growing retiree community an hour from Metro Manila.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. Note: Taal Volcano is active — the 2020 eruption displaced thousands. Retirees should be aware of volcanic risk and ash events.
cities.healthcare_notes: Tagaytay Medical Center and private clinics provide basic care; Manila hospitals are 1.5–2 hours for specialist care.
best_for: Those wanting cooler temperatures within Philippine access
Scenic highland living seekers
Those wanting proximity to Manila without the chaos
avoid_if: Those concerned about volcanic activity (Taal erupted 2020)
Those needing immediate specialist medical access

cities.description: Luzon's southern gateway — Anilao's world-class macro-diving, Laiya Beach, affordable provincial living, and a stepping stone to Palawan and Mindoro by ferry.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. Batangas offers a quieter, more authentic Philippine provincial lifestyle compared to Manila or Cebu.
cities.healthcare_notes: Batangas Medical Center and St. Patrick's Hospital provide local care. Manila is 2–3 hours for specialist needs.
best_for: Divers (Anilao is one of Asia's best macro-diving spots)
Those wanting quiet provincial Philippines
Ferry travelers to the Visayas and Mindoro
avoid_if: Those wanting urban amenities
Retirees needing specialist medical access locally

cities.description: The heart of Philippine civility — consistently ranked the Philippines' most liveable city, a university hub, gateway to pristine Guimaras island, and a proud culinary tradition of batchoy and fresh seafood.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. Iloilo is often overlooked by expats — resulting in lower costs and a more authentic local experience than Cebu or Manila.
cities.healthcare_notes: Western Visayas Medical Center and St. Paul's Hospital provide solid regional care with multiple universities maintaining hospital-quality standards.
best_for: Those wanting the Philippines' most liveable provincial city
Culinary enthusiasts
Those wanting Guimaras island proximity (best mangoes in the world)
avoid_if: Those wanting the Philippines' broadest urban amenity range

cities.description: The City of Smiles — Negros Occidental's sugar capital, MassKara Festival masks, the Philippines' chicken inasal capital, affordable real estate, and a genuinely warm and hospitable community.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. Bacolod is one of the Philippines' most underrated cities for retirement — lower costs than Cebu or Iloilo with a warm local community.
cities.healthcare_notes: Corazon Locsin Montelibano Memorial Regional Hospital and Riverside Medical Center provide solid care.
best_for: Budget retirees wanting a genuine Philippine city experience
Culinary explorers
MassKara Festival lovers
avoid_if: Those wanting international-level amenities
Retirees needing a large established expat community

cities.description: Mindanao's gateway and fastest-growing city — whitewater rafting on the Cagayan River, gateway to Camiguin island, a booming commercial hub, and Mindanao's safest major city.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. CDO is outside the typhoon belt and serves as the commercial hub for northern Mindanao and gateway to Camiguin and Bukidnon highlands.
cities.healthcare_notes: Northern Mindanao Medical Center and Capitol University Medical Center provide solid regional care.
best_for: Adventure retirees (whitewater rafting, Camiguin volcano-island)
Budget Mindanao seekers
avoid_if: Those who conflate all of Mindanao's reputation with CDO (it is genuinely safe)
Retirees wanting beach-front city living

cities.description: The Philippines' tuna capital and Manny Pacquiao's hometown — South Cotabato's fast-growing city, the world's most active tuna port, affordable living, and a proud southern Mindanao identity.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. GenSan is the Philippines' southernmost major city and perhaps its most underrated — extremely low costs and a self-sufficient provincial city.
cities.healthcare_notes: Mindanao Central Referral Hospital and GenSan Doctors Hospital provide regional care.
best_for: Ultra-budget retirees
Seafood lovers (tuna straight from the dock)
Those wanting the Philippines' most off-the-beaten-path large city
avoid_if: Those wanting expat community or international amenities
Those not comfortable with the Philippines' deep south geography

cities.description: The Asia of Asia — a unique Spanish-Chavacano culture, pink sand beaches at Santa Cruz islands, vintas (colorful outriggers), and an authentic city that few foreign retirees have discovered.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. The security situation in Zamboanga and the broader BARMM region requires careful research — periodic incidents have historically occurred though the city itself is often secure.
cities.healthcare_notes: Zamboanga City Medical Center and Brent Hospital provide local care. Serious cases transfer to Cebu or Manila.
best_for: Adventurous retirees intrigued by Chavacano Spanish-creole culture
Those wanting the Philippines' most unique city
avoid_if: Those with safety concerns (security situation requires research)
Retirees wanting established expat infrastructure

cities.description: The Philippines' most beloved expat retirement city — Silliman University's tree-lined campus, whale shark and thresher shark diving at Oslob and Apo Island, and a genuinely liveable small city.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. Dumaguete is consistently ranked among the Philippines' top retirement destinations for foreign retirees — a well-earned reputation.
cities.healthcare_notes: Silliman University Medical Center and Holy Child Hospital provide solid care. Cebu is 45 minutes by fast ferry for advanced specialist care.
best_for: Divers (Apo Island is world-class, Oslob whale sharks)
Those wanting the Philippines' best small-city expat community
University-town atmosphere seekers
avoid_if: Those wanting big-city amenities
Retirees needing major hospital access locally

cities.description: The Philippines' best-preserved Spanish colonial city and a UNESCO World Heritage site — cobblestoned Calle Crisologo, kalesa horse-drawn carriages, longganisa sausages, and an Ilocos Norte cultural heritage unlike anywhere else in Asia.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. Vigan is 8 hours north of Manila by bus — somewhat remote for a retirement base but extraordinary for cultural tourism.
cities.healthcare_notes: Gabriela Silang General Hospital provides basic care; serious cases go to Laoag (30 min) or Manila (8 hrs).
best_for: History and colonial architecture lovers
Those wanting an extraordinarily unique and affordable Philippine city
Heritage tourism enthusiasts
avoid_if: Those wanting urban amenities or a large expat community
Retirees needing specialist healthcare locally

cities.description: The Philippines' Summer Capital — Luzon's cool highland city at 1,500m, pine trees, strawberry farms, Camp John Hay, and a refreshing escape from the tropical heat that has drawn Filipinos and expats for a century.
cities.visa_notes: Philippines SRRV applies. Baguio's cooler climate (avg 18°C) is the primary draw — making it the Philippines' most comfortable city for those from temperate countries.
cities.healthcare_notes: Baguio General Hospital and Medical Center and Saint Louis University Hospital provide solid regional care.
best_for: Those who find tropical heat oppressive
Highland lifestyle and cool climate lovers
Philippine summer capital culture enthusiasts
avoid_if: Those wanting beach access (inland, mountainous)
Retirees sensitive to traffic (Baguio's narrow mountain roads get very congested)
